ops.title: Holder mount contraindications: attrcache + scheduling band (proven)
ops.body:
Two proven constraints on how holder mounts may be served. Full narratives live here; code
keeps one-line pointers.

## 1. AttrCache is contraindicated for synth-document mounts (VM gate, 2026-07-03, 2/2 failures)

A mount serving SYNTHETIC DOCUMENTS whose size changes and are promptly re-read — cc-pool's
merged .claude.json shape, vmstress's synth envelope — tears in BOTH directions under any
nonzero go-nfsv4 attrcache TTL: a stale-small cached size clamps a grown document mid-JSON, and
a stale-large size over-reads a shrunk one into NUL padding. v0.23.0 attr stabilization does not
help: the size legitimately changes on every rewrite, and go-nfsv4 has no invalidation path from
the fs. Such consumers must never opt in. scripts/vm/scenarios/validate-attrcache.sh is the
regression gate for any future attempt; the gate failed 2/2 within seconds of churn.

## 2. The Darwin background band is contraindicated for the holder process tree (live, 2026-07-03)

v0.23.0 demoted the holder via setpriority(PRIO_DARWIN_PROCESS, 0, PRIO_DARWIN_BG), inherited by
the per-mount go-nfsv4 servers. The band = CPU throttle + lowest disk I/O tier — but this
process tree is the data plane for every consumer fs syscall on the mounts. Measured live on a
loaded 10-mount host (cc-pool fleet, load ~25): 1.4-3.4ms open/close vs a ~214µs normal-priority
control floor (an independently spawned go-nfsv4 at PRI 31), "serves metadata but hangs reads"
mirror wedges, holder-saturation liveness failures, and client processes burning 400%+ CPU in
syscall system time. A self-set band is UNCLEARABLE from outside the process: taskpolicy -B and
setpriority(PRIO_DARWIN_PROCESS, pid, 0) both return success without effect (verified with a
controlled experiment on a scratch self-BG process) — affected holders can only be cured by
restart. The politeness intent survives as proc.Nice(5) (classic nice: soft weight, untouched
I/O, no starvation cliff; one-way for unprivileged processes).

Original demotion rationale, for the record: a detached child launched via `open -g` has no
LaunchAgent plist, so launchd's Nice/ProcessType keys cannot apply; demoting in-process is the
only lever — the lever is right, the band was wrong.
